Answering these questions leads to four different kinds of problem attention styles that are always at play when policy makers and advocates seek to address policy problems. Type 1 captures those that do seek to enhance some kind of utility outcome governing a problem at hand – such as say restaurant workers who, addicted to their phones, do not give customers attention they would need to return a second time, but do not take into consideration whether solving that problem might create other problems – such as say – banning the use of phones during working hours that make it difficult for mothers to communicate with child care workers the number of hours that are needed on a particular day. What is most novel about this framework, and what sets it apart from existing work, is that it targets the ways in which each school treats "whack-a-mole" effects: i.e. the phenomenon in which successfully addressing one problem makes others worse. While the sustainability governance literature often refers to these issues as "trade-offs", I call for a much more nuanced approach that recognizes distinct, but largely hidden ways, in which each of these schools' deeply rich and sophisticated ethical obligations provide different ways to conceive of, and address, inevitable moles. It also shows that despite limited attention to moles, these schools promote strong synergies biases when confronted with whack-a-mole effects, which results in redesigning, or creating new interventions, to uncover them, rather than confronting their problem conception biases. I show how all schools recognize, and have well developed internally coherent adjudicating frameworks, for whacking moles. However, they are silent on the *external moles* that pop up when successfully and effectively managing their school's approach to sustainability outcomes. Uncovering these external moles reveals how the Commons, Optimization and Compromise schools *limit* an ability to ameliorating Type 4 problems to those that are *synergistic* with their preferred Type. That is, they implicitly advance an approach to problem solving that does not simply ask how innovative policies might be developed to ameliorate climate and species extinctions, but *whether*, given each school's adjudicating framework, it is rational or feasible to do so. This helps explain the eyebrow raising conclusion of leading sustainability governance scholars that, despite widespread evidence that effective policies governing sustainability goals championed by Commons, Optimization and Compromise schools *caused* climate and species extinctions moles (Nilsson and Weitz 2019b; Independent Group of Scientists 2019; Khamila et al. 2022), synergies among environmental, social and economic goals "far exceeds the number of trade-offs" (IPCC 2018, sec. 2.5.3) and that, where whack-a-mole effects do occur, they can be converted into synergies through careful design of transition portfolios (IPCC 2018, 33). In contrast, the Prioritization school's internal approach to adjudicating moles does not leave it to chance that synergies can be found with other sustainability goals and objectives. Instead, it fosters an analytical technique that begins, and ends, with lexical ordering: i.e., its starting point is that second order moles that result from successfully managing higher ranked first order problems must be treated *sequentially*, and only in ways that do not undermine higher ranked problems. Uncovering these differences is profoundly important because they make clear that only the Prioritization school's adjudicating framework is "fit for purpose" for identifying effective and unconditional solutions for ameliorating the ecological effects of climate change, species extinctions, and, for that matter, the loss of indigenous peoples' traditional livelihoods. In sum, all four schools are motivated by deeply entrenched, but competing, ethical foundations (Cashore and Bernstein 2022) that underpin each school's transformative policy design project (Visseren-Hamakers et al. 2023). These ethics, in turn, carry profound implications for how to think about, and address, inevitable synergies and "whack-a-mole"^1 effects (Levin et al. 2012), that can result from a highly effective policy design. Notably, the Compromise, Optimization and Commons schools narrow the ability to address problems conceived of through a Type 4 lens to those that are synergistic with Types 3, 2 and 1 conceptions (Cashore and Bernstein 2022). The framework has been applied to show how these (often) unconscious biases, and the metaphors they produce, can result in policies that are inconsistent with the scientific evidence about behavioural changes necessary for effectively managing critical problems including the climate and biodiversity crises. Section 16: ##### **i. Commons (Type 1 reinforcing)** ***Problem solving? Yes.*** The Commons school is focused on designing "collective action" institutions capable of avoiding "tragedies of the commons" in which overharvesting causes catastrophic resource collapse. It draws on decades of attention understand and resolve "collective action" dilemmas (Goldstein and Lenway 1989) in which owing to absent or ineffective governance institutions individual utility enhancing "rational" behaviour in the short term leads to utility undermining "irrational" behaviour in the long run for both individuals and the broader community in which they are situated. Collective action scholars apply their lens to understand and manage "on the ground" problems from "rubber necking" on highways that cause utility undermining traffic jams, to the hoarding of toilet paper during pandemics (Cashore and Bernstein 2020). The role of collective action theory in sustainability studies was made famous by Garret Hardin (1968) most famously labelled these as "tragedies of the commons" which turned attention to goods such as grasslands, fish and timber, that have "subtractability" as their key structural feature: i.e. their use by one individual takes away the ability of another to also use that resource. This interest led Commons school has applied the collective action metaphor most notably in sustainability studies to the case of avoiding, or ameliorating, uncoordinated "open access" situations (Araral 2014) in which overharvesting leads to resource collapse. For these reasons they thought carefully about the types of institutional arrangements most capable of excluding access. This forms the bedrock of this schools approach, determining every other decision and approach. Initially and following Garret Hardin's famous essay generated widespread interest in finding ways to overcome "subtractability": i.e. those cases in which the use of a resource undermines the ability of another to use it. Ostrom would make profound innovative contributions by turning to conceptualizations of specific on the ground problems that for– for either biophysical reasons (such as the ability of fish to swim long distances) or traditional community practices (Gibson, McKean, and Ostrom 2000) – as having features that made it impossible or difficult for policy makers to exclude access (Ostrom and Ostrom 1972). ***Utility dominates? Yes.*** Overcoming these undesirable conditions has been reinforced by a number of game theoretic metaphors (Nash 1953), most notably the "Prisoners dilemma", in which two thieves being interrogated in different cells and unable to coordinate make the utility chasing "rational" choice, to confess to even though collective silence would have produced lower aggregate prison terms. Hence collective action theory in general, and its application to sustainability studies in particular, begins, and ends, with the championing of optimal utility outcomes ***Solution orientation:*** two overall approaches emerged initially within this school: either privatizing land to individuals (who would now have, it was posited, individual incentives to produce long term utility enhancing benefits), or to turn to coercive state central authority and associated enforcement mechanisms. Ostrom would make profound innovative contributions by noting that for– for either biophysical reasons (such as the ability of fish to swim long distances) or traditional community practices (Gibson, McKean, and Ostrom 2000) – as having features that made it impossible or difficult for policy makers to exclude access (Ostrom 1990). Formal incorporation of "excludability" yielded not one but four ways to classify, and label, economically suboptimal resource depletion challenges: subtractable and excludable "private goods"; subtractable but not excludable common pool resources; non-subtractable and excludable "club goods", and non-subtractable, non-excludable "public goods". Although many of Ostrom's students self-label today as environmental governance scholars, there is no dispute that GTC's focus, and design principles, were explicitly derived from economic sustainability challenges (Ostrom 1990, 3). However, what is interesting, and long forgotten, is that although her work would largely reinforce Type 1 problems, she saw an equally compelling need to engage in similar attention to Type 4 problem structures for those seeking design insights for ameliorating the degradation of ecological and biological systems (Ostrom 1990, 1). > Table 1:2 Conception of Resource Goods | Goods Category | Subtractable?

Commons and sustainability scholars continue to differ on whether to treat trees, fish, and ecosystems as “private goods”, “common pool resources” and, in some cases, as “public” or “club” goods. These debates can be traced back, in part, to different ways in which categories and corresponding solutions are constructed (Young 2007: 3-4). For example, following Ostrom’s work in *Governing the Commons*, some view institutions, depending on their design, as causing or solving, a particular resource depletion tragedy (i.e. they constitute the “independent” variable). However, others view institutional arrangements, not simply biophysical features, as playing a role in making excludability difficult (i.e. they constitute part of the “dependent” variable). It follows that if a researcher deems institutions to have played Ostrom also made it clear in GTC, and subsequently, that she believed that the tool of privatization, rather than coercive state control, was the most optimal for managing "strictly" private goods (Ostrom 2003, 259; Gibson, McKean, and Ostrom 2000, 7).^1 Her concern was that the widespread application of the privatization solution to include non-excludable CPR type commons tragedies drew on "special models that utilize extreme assumptions" (Ostrom 1990, 183) that could produce even more tragic outcomes than if nothing had been done at all (Ostrom 1990, 13-14) such as the expropriation of communal forests that had historically avoided resource collapse (Bartley et al. 2008; Gibson, McKean, and Ostrom 2000). GTC further posited that local communal governance institutions, including "groups of individuals", rather than "distant" central authorities, were best poised to avoid or reverse, many types of unsustainable CPR practices (Ostrom 1990, 21-23, 60-61, 183-185). While this approach helps account for today's "subsidiarity" metaphor among development scholars, similar inductive reasoning however has led the commons school, guided by the "polycentric" metaphor, to advocate for the "scaling up" of its design principles to ameliorate economically sub-optimal "global commons" challenges that cut across multiple jurisdictions.^1 ***Ethical motivations:*** researchers are often motivated by an expressed moral duty to help avoid the devastating effects that the collapse of an economically beneficial resource wreaks on human beings, especially resource dependent communities. To do this the guiding moral concern is to overcome these suboptimal outcomes through the development of collective institutional arrangements. Ostrom championed the primacy of this school in her presidential address to the American Political Science Association, arguing that: "… the theory of collective action is the central subject of political science" (Ostrom 1998, 1). ***Dominant disciplines:*** the discipline of economics forms the bedrock foundations for the Commons school, given its focus on utility enhancing management of natural resources. At the same time, especially following Ostrom, the focus on community governance as a preferred solution has drawn interest from anthropologists, and cousins in geography and sociological whose grounded theory methods provide necessary nuance for developing effective locally specific institutions. ***Dominant methodologies:*** the search for theories to inform particular Type 1 resource depletion tragedies – helps explain the commons school embracing of game theoretic models, and quantitative methods required to build them. Qualitative ethnographic methods play a supporting role in so far as their insights help improve model building (Brondizio, Ostrom, and Young 2009; Araral 2014, 12; Andersson, Evans, and Richards 2009). ***Theory building project:*** commons scholars and general, and Ostrom in particular, are clear that they are motivated to initiate "*better policies*" (Ostrom 1990, 29) for averting, or avoiding, depleting water, fish, timber, and other goods that contribute to human food systems, required "arranging for the supply of new institutions" (Ostrom 1990, 14) that must be placed on "how *best* to limit the use of natural resources so as to ensure their long-term *economic viability*" (Ostrom 1990, 1). Ostrom offered that getting the utility enhancing "institutions right" requiring identifying and granting "user rights", reducing "free-riding" risks and "commitment problems". She cautioned that successful efforts in "monitoring individual compliance with sets of rules" (Ostrom 1990, 27) would be "a difficult, time-consuming, conflict-invoking process" (Ostrom 1990, 14). ***Birds and moles:*** following Ostrom's seminal contribution, commons school scholars are now keenly aware of, and think deeply about avoiding, the devastating internal "whack-a-mole" effects that can occur when treating all commons tragedies as excludable. Less attention has been placed on three external whack-a-mole effects that fall outside its moral, theoretical and conceptual foundations. First, successfully targeting a specific good could result in an economically suboptimal Type 2 outcome if converting the land to a different commodity or use – such as mineral extraction, manufacturing, real estate or even tourism – would have produced higher society wide aggregate economic utility. Second, while the Commons School certainly turned to the importance of local cultural practices for their role in fostering collective long-term Type 1 sustainability in the absence of formal institutions (Ostrom 1990, 149, 166; Sethi and Somanathan 1996), it does not formally incorporate into its model historical cultural traditions that fall outside of, or are degraded by, an emphasis on economic utility (Buck 1998). Third, and most important for our analysis, negative environmental effects can, and usually do, result from the solving a resource depletion tragedy, including, for example, the ecologically degrading effects of sustained yield logging and fishing on forest and marine ecosystems. To be sure, Commons scholars have certainly considered the impact of utility enhancing frameworks on environmental degradation. However, arguably owing to this school's deeply entrenched moral foundations, such reflexive exercises tend to advance causal beliefs that Type 1 and Type 4 problems are synergistic (Prakash and Gupta ; Costanza 2006). While this school is sophisticated about what to do to solve a collective action challenge, it does not provide intellectual guidance about which problem, from sustained yields of timber, to exotic crops from soy to palm to eucalyptus, to fisheries to local water management, ought to be given attention. This means that if effectively addressed, the solution may result in synergies and moles that were not part of the inductive reasoning that led to managing the specified Type 1 problem at hand. For example, managing fisheries as a Type 1 problem may, depending on the reproduction logic of the resource in question, require maintaining the entire structure and function of the ecosystem itself—even though that is not the concern of Type 1 problem frames. Interestingly the main recommendations from the 2005 economics of water report (Ovink 2024)– that was produced to mirror the Stern review (2007)on the Type 2 economics of climate and Dasgupta review (2021)on the Type 2 economics of biodiversity essentially concluded that if Type 1 water management across the world was to be conserved, the Type 4 ecological structure and function of forests and biodiversity needed to be maintained. However, the reverse can also be true, such as when successfully managing for the highest Type 1 sustained yields are found to undermine ecosystem system structure and function and/or render extinct particular species (Cashore and Howlett 2007). Likewise, the Commons school is largely silent on what to do when successfully designing institutional arrangements for managing a particular good in the long run might undermine an ability to convert the land to a more lucrative crop, such as palm oil or soy plantations, that would have provided even higher utility enhancing benefit (Cashore and Bernstein 2022). ***Effectiveness:*** For all these reasons the Common's school is anchored in achieving desired policy objectives facing a clearly specified, and measurable, "on the ground" challenge whether it is about maintaining the long-term supply of utility enhancing fish for a poverty stricken community, timber management in 1000s of forest dependent communities across India, or long term supplies of clean water facing local urban areas, there is no success, and no sense of accomplishment, if the specified issue at hand becomes degraded or collapsed. This is important because its attention on revisiting theories and prescriptions if they don't cause the resource good to be maintained (rather than simply managing degradation more slowly) is not only a critically important aspect of this School for Sustainability scholarship policy and governance, its approach to "on the ground" effectiveness in managing the particular Type 1 problem at hand, can, and must, be emulated by Type 4 problems that consumer the interest, and attention, of the Prioritization school. In sum, this school's problem orientation is decidedly not focused on ecological, but economic outcomes. As Ostrom made clear, "The issue in this case—and many others—is how best to limit the use of natural resources so as to ensure their long-term *economic viability*" (Ostrom 1990: 1). Section 17: ##### **ii. Optimization (Type 2 reinforcing)** ***Problem solving? No.*** Those who belong to this school of sustainability thought and practice do not justify their analytical frames based on key features of a specified problem. Instead, they are united by developing approaches to thinking about, researching, and identifying solutions for managing any kind of problem that might reach the societal or policy agendas. They are united by theories and approaches that do not derive from the specific features of a particular kind of problem, but for developing universal approaches to address not only how, but whether, a problem can and ought to be effectively managed. To be sure, the key features of any "on the ground" problem at hand are certainly not ignored, but they do not constrain or narrow the universal theory building project about how to understanding satisfaction utility enhancing behaviours on the part of individuals, organizations or states. They may use an on the ground case to generate a universal theory, but the general theory will be applies beyond the case to to explain behaviour in general, and to identity and narrow policy solutions ***Utility dominates? Yes.*** Whereas the commons school champions a utility maximizing outcome to manage a clearly specified collective action problem, the optimization school advances the moral belief that any problem at hand must only be solved if solutions enhance the utility of individuals, organizations and states at the aggregate level (i.e. they can only effectively manage a particular problem at hand if doing so enhances satisfactions overall. If satisfactions are undermined overall by solving one problem in isolation, they develop implicit and explicit frames and methodologies that reinforce a morally inappropriate do solve a problem at hand if it undermines overall aggregate utility of individuals, organizations and states. The major strand dominating sustainability policy and governance within this school are those who advance environmental economics. This group generally advances CBA by drawing neo-classical or behavioural techniques nurtured within the economics discipline. For this group, if a solution cannot be found to the specific issue at hand that doesn't result in the undermining of overall aggregate utility- what is also referred to as "social welfare", then the "rational" answer is that it cannot be solved. It is for these reasons that this school often advances a "partial" solution that is not in line with the problem at hand but does do something. Hence the Optimization school shares an interest with the Commons school in advancing some kind of utility or satisfaction for humans and their organization but differs in that it does not just address how to solve a problem at hand, but whether it is "rational" to do so. ***Solution orientation:*** For these reasons the solution is around maximizing satisfactions over all, which will determine not only how, but whether the problem at hand can be addressed. ***Ethical motivations:*** Within sustainability studies, this school's moral framework provided the dominant rationale for the creation of forestry, agriculture, and resource schools in Europe and North America that were motivated by utilitarian principles^7 of "the greatest good for the greatest number in the long run" (Pinchot 1987), as well as most post-WWII development agencies, including the World Bank. Of course, these are deeply held. To be sure, specific strands have all developed deeply ethical justifications. For example. The economic optimization strand of this school advances the moral belief that the quest for policy solutions that enhance aggregate economic utility is the best outcome for society as a whole (Kenny 2011; Sen 1979; Luke 2009). In contrast the international relations strand focuses on understanding why states cooperate, and based on that answer, to identifying, and helping create, the most "optimal" institutions to achieving the highest level of preferences. To be sure, the massive debate within this strand about whether relative or absolute gains motivate states to engage in international cooperation reinforce the universalist nature of this project that is disconnected from, but meant to be applied to, any policy that might come on the geo-political agenda. ***Dominant disciplines:*** Economics, rational and public choice political scientists, international relations scholars including neo-realists (emphasizing relative gains) and neo-liberal institutionalist (emphasizing absolute gains). ***Dominant methodologies:*** The nature of this school's quest for a universal theory to explain the empirical world they are interested in, from aggregate economic utility to optimal preferencing building state cooperation, means that quantitative methods are, and must, be, dominant. These include econometrics modelling and game theoretic analyses. The most dominant is "cost-benefit" analysis (Adler and Posner 2009; Arrow et al. 1996) in which disparate social, environmental, cultural and economic goals are given comparable economic utility values. The field of environmental economics advances this method based on their causal belief that the rational and feasible way to address environmental challenges is to convert them into economic values (Stavins 1995; Sachs et al. 2020; Thomas and Chindarkar 2019). This approach, which underpins the US Environmental Protection Agency's policy to value each statistical human life at $9.47 million USD (Environmental Protection Agency 2020) involves modelling across a range of outcomes, as well as discounting future economic impacts, to identify the "best" or most "rational" policy options. Internal debates – including whether policies must be avoided that increase economic inequality (Kaldor 1939) – whether measured as widening gaps in relative gains (Piketty 2015) or Pareto losses (Awan 2013) - reinforce the school's overarching emphasis on utility enhancing orientations by narrowing consideration of inequality to economic measures. Other methods for giving utility enhancing equivalents include the production of quantitative surveys of consumers and the public's "willingness to pay" so that concerns about species extinctions, wilderness, and the catastrophic ecological effects of climate change can be converted into economic values (Turner et al. 2003) ***Theory building project:*** arguably the most dominant of these strands today is economics which advances the causal belief that the rational and feasible way to address environmental challenges is to convert them into economic values (Nordhaus 1991; Stavins 1995; Sachs et al. 2020; Thomas and Chindarkar 2019). Drawing explicitly or implicitly on welfare economics,^1 that the ability to solve any kind of problem is conditional upon finding policy solutions that enhance aggregate economic utility for society as a whole (Kenny 2011; Sen 1979; Luke 2009). This school of thought and practice also includes international relations sustainability scholarship on cooperation and rational institutional whose sophisticated analytical frameworks are designed to extend beyond, and are not derived from, the structural features of the environmental problem at hand. Rather, they generate their underlying theories from attempts to understand state cooperation generally that apply to any and all problems and from that, identify possible solutions to the problem at hand, such as climate change (e.g.Keohane 2016; e.g.Keohane and Victor 2016; Ovodenko and Keohane 2012; Koremenos 2016). To be clear, this does not mean that these scholars do not care about identifying possible solutions to the Type 4 environmental climate, biodiversity and forests crises – many of them do. Rather, their ability to do so in not derived from the key features of the problem at hand, but their theories of international cooperation.^8 It also applies to rational choice strands within political science who seek to understand ways to construct and maintain governance institutions capable of enhancing aggregate utility. ***Effectiveness:*** This school of thought therefore is not about achieving an environmental outcome qua and environmental outcome – such as say, doing what biologists say is required for averting a species from becoming extinct – but instead by whether this problem was handled in a way that maintain aggregate utility or some other equivalent, such a enhancing state preferences, whether based on absolute or relative gains. Hence, and given this school's ethical beliefs, it turns to empirical measures of aggregate utility, and corresponding asserted benefits for society that will accrue including poverty alleviation, directly and indirectly. Direct benefits include higher levels of employment and income. Indirect benefits include enhanced resources for health care, schools, and other social programs that can produce and improve the human conditions (Rawls and Laband 2004). This approach of this school has been credited with generating effective policy designs that have lifted millions out of poverty (Hasan 2021). ***Implicit biases:*** Adherents to the optimization school relay on utility maximization to assess not only how, but whether, it is rational to any particular problem that comes across the desk of a policy official, including climate and biodiversity. ***Birds and moles:*** For these reasons the Optimization school is devoted to bringing in as many problems as possible into their research and applied analysis so that policy officials do not inadvertently make a "suboptimal" choice owing to lack of information. This allows them to focus both on "positive" (birds) and "negative" (moles) externalities. This schools' approach certainly can, and does, lead to multiple tentacles associated with utility maximization such as greater community stability, lower levels of violence, and greater access to education and health care that often result from increasing income- even though the extent to which these will occurs will always be uncertain. The CBA strand addresses moles as unintended "externalities" that must be measured by giving these outcomes utility undermining values – usually through "willingness to pay" surveys – and then incorporating this into their models as a way to "internalizing" these "externalities". This approach is reinforced by their ethical foundations, and causal beliefs, that valuing nature economically will provide ancillary environmental benefits (Sell et al. 2007). This morality is also behind the "polluter pays" metaphor (Díaz-Reviriego, Turnhout, and Beck 2019b; OECD 1975, 1994) which holds that firms can chose to stop an environmentally degrading behaviour if the (economic) costs to the firm of continuing are too high, or it can continue polluting if the costs are deemed low and behind their turning to policies that encourage "payment for ecosystem *services*" (PES) (Sell et al. 2007). At the same time this school's approach to address moles by giving them utility equivalents, can, and does, create new Type 1, 2 and 4 moles that neo-classical and behaviour economics in general, and the CBA strand in particular, rarely think about, let alone address . In a classic and disturbing example to members of the Prioritization school, (Type 2 reinforcing) economist William Nordhaus' has concluded, based on his sophisticated modelling of the economics of climate degradation^9, conclusion that that the most optimal, or "economically rational" future are world temperatures at 3.2°C warming above natural levels (Nordhaus 2017) despite (Type 4 reinforcing) scientific evidence produce by the world's leading scientists that 1.5/2°C is the maximum warming possible without risking catastrophic ecological changes (Cashore and Bernstein 2022; IPCC 2018). Hence, in Nordhaus' case even though he is focused on the climate crisis he is not problem contingent: i.e. he did not develop a theory and approach to solving the climate crisis at 1.5 or 2 °C. Rather he applied a pre-existing framework focused on maximizing aggregate utility that determines both how, and whether, a problem can be "rationally" solved. Keohane, chasing a theory of state cooperation based on absolute gains, takes the same overall ontological approach, albeit applying his own theories and methods. Hence, while this school is sophisticated at internalizing a range of outcomes into utility enhancing models – it pays no attention to, nor does it accept as addressable (Rawls and Laband 2004), moles that this school creates. Most notably, its emphasis on economic growth can, through resulting industrialization processes, result in the homogenization of previously diverse local cultures, while associated increases in consumption can significantly stress the earth's environmental "carrying capacity." Similarly, its emphasis on existing "willingness to pay" calculations to convert environmental values into equivalent economic utility comparators, undermines reflexive considerations about the role of norm generation that may shift how humans conceive of ecological systems – such as viewing them not as providing services to humans to but as places worth of protection in their own right: i.e., conserving ecological systems qua ecological systems. In the most extreme, some rational choice political scientists have gone so far as to treat Type 1 and 2 human satisfying outcomes not only as synergistic, but as synonymous, with Type 4 environmental concerns (e.g. Lomborg 2001). Section 18: ##### **iii. Compromise (Type 3 reinforcing)** ***Problem solving? No.*** The compromise school seeks to generate legitimate and trustworthy processes through which multiple perspectives and concerns are included when deliberating not only how, but whether, to ameliorate any given problem at hand. Like the optimization school the quest is not to develop a stakeholder process based on the specific features of the problem at hand and working backwards to find an effective policy solution, but rather, to apply a pre-existing approach to deliberations that the public, stakeholders, and policy makers deem to be appropriate. ***Utility dominates? No.*** This problem framing have been prominent at the international level since 1987 Brundtland Commission (WCED 1987), whose championing of a "three legged stool" of sustainability, as an *objective* way to achieve *sustainable outcomes*, permitted a bridge conversation with, but then sparked an "outside" critique of, the economic optimization school. At first, the Brundtland report articulated a now widely diffused a Type 2 anthropocentric moral imperative that sustainability required "meeting the needs of the current generation without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs". However, this framing would subtly but powerfully confront the economic optimization strand's narrowing of environmental and social problems to their utility enhancing benefits (not to mention that most in this strand, until Stern, accepted as objective, the subjective belief that longer term Type 2 outcomes must be "discounted" compared to immediate ones). These tensions led to a second metaphor post Brundtland in which a conversation with the Prioritization school (Wang 2004) led to the incorporation the impacts of humans on the natural environment on the belief that sustainability must incorporate, but treat as distinct, environmental, social and economic goals through the "three legged" stool metaphor. ***Solution orientation:*** the Compromise school incorporates, but expands, an emphasis on utility to include, in their own right, environmental, social, and cultural goals (Cashore and Bernstein 2022). Its ethical foundations, highlighted by the Brundtland Report (WCED 1987) are aimed at promoting balance among competing goals and interests through principles of inclusiveness, transparency, accountability, and empowerment (Cashore and Nathan 2020). ***Ethical motivations:*** the compromise school's moral philosophy rests on the belief that when a specific "on the ground" problem comes on the policy agenda it is morally appropriate and fair to engage a range of ideas, interests and stakeholders with diverse values and concerns, including marginalized and vulnerable communities, groups, and people. This school's moral foundations from a rich literature from pluralism to tri-partite corporatism that is devoted to understanding, and building "good governance" at multiple scales through enhancing legitimacy and trust (Dahl 1961a; Rosenbluth and Shapiro 2018; Bodansky 1999; Habermas 1973; Scharpf 1999; March and Olsen 1998). Various stands of this school articulate there mission in specific ways, such as the Lasswellian "policy sciences" community united around the promotion of "human dignity" rather than economic utility (Farr, Hacker, and Kazee 2006). Within sustainability studies, these themes are reflected in a rich set of scholarship aimed at understanding, and assessing, how inclusionary processes and deliberative spaces might foster meaningful involvement of disempowered actors, generate new ideas (Dryzek 1990; Hoberg 1992; Pinkerton 1993), and perhaps improve distributional considerations (Aklin and Mildenberger 2017). ***Dominant disciplines:*** include political science (especially strands on legitimacy, state-society interaction), sociology, psychology, anthropology, economics ***Dominant methodologies:*** for this school require, out of necessity, mixed approaches including of quantitative and qualitative analysis. Special attention has been placed on insights for generating consensus among environment and development "advocacy coalitions" (Sabatier 1988). Applied policy analyses turns to some type of "multi-goal" policy analysis (Weimer and Vining 1992) and stakeholder deliberations to achieve consensus and/or "dispute resolution." Significant research is conducted to assess how representatives of different interests or values might be incorporated that avoids the tendency to drift towards Type 2 (Raymond and DeNardis 2015; Clémençon 2012). Those scholars in the applied space include turn to "multi-disciplinary" or "interdisciplinary" knowledge generation owing to causal and moral belief that integrating a diversity of perspectives *will* yield the most legitimate and effective policy responses (Nilsson and Weitz 2019a; Saez and Requena 2007; Clark and Wallace 2011). This strand of the compromise school includes an important network interdisciplinary collaborations with natural scientists – including some trained in Ostrom's Indiana workshop – aimed at promoting Bruntdlandesque morality as a *scientific* endeavour (Schmitz 2018; Reed et al. 2019; Matson, Clark, and Andersson 2016; Glaser and Bates 2011). These efforts culminated when the US National Academy of Sciences - whose members strongly bias, and reward, quantitative research- defined and formalized the compromise school's subjective conception of "sustainability science" as "…the interactions between natural and social systems, and with how those interactions affect the challenge of sustainability: meeting the needs of present and future generations while substantially reducing poverty and conserving the planet's life support systems" (Kates 2011, 19449). This, in turn, would pave the way for many sustainability scholars to present their subjective Type 3 cognitive frames as objective by making universalist claims that their scientific research *found* that sustainability solutions *required* compromise be reached among disparate groups (Jacobsen and Linnell 2016; Grove and Forster 2011; Bryant and Jackson 1999; Baker et al. 2009). Practitioners and policy officials would draw on these conclusions to justify as "scientific" their quest to elaborate the "foundations" (Starik 1995) of "multiple-use" land management (Shands 1988). These moral frames shape research agendas, including a plethora of mostly large-N quantitative studies in scientific journals including the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, but they would be drawn upon by business interests and associations, such as the World Business Council on Sustainable Development (WBCSD), to champion and fund research and teaching by the compromise and economic optimization schools. They would also be drawn on, as the owl case reviewed in Part III illustrates, to sparked, and reinforced, a plethora of research into the conditions through multi-stakeholder processes might end the "war in the woods" by collectively defining, and practice "sustainable" resources and forest management (Davis 1992; Breyman 1993; Dietrich 1993; Gup 1990; Hogarth 1991) which was identified as in opposition to "elitist" Type 4 species conservation policies that were bureaucratic, anti-community and, was sometimes asserted, anti-democratic. A much more subtle, but arguably even more powerful strand of this school was articulated by US and global scholars who, following their concerns about conflicts over debates about land use among environmental activists, indigenous peoples, resource dependent communities, labourers, and businesses sought to understand better the processes through which respectful dialogue might lead to legitimate solutions aimed at avoiding conflict and producing consensus through compromise (see, for example, Halbert and Lee 1990; Wondolleck 1988; Crowfoot and Wondolleck 1991; Bacow and Wheeler 1984; Wilkinson and Anderson 1985; Bingham 1986; Pinkerton 1993; Armitage et al. 2009; Kelly and Alper 1995; Coglianese 1997; Beyers 2001; Brach et al. 2002; Koontz and Thomas 2006). ***Theory building project:*** turns to understanding, or constructing, governance arenas that, through particular ways to foster deliberative decisions making will lead to them being evaluated as so appropriate that its authority is accepted, even if a particular decision (Scharpf 2000a),at any one point is not consistent with what a particular groups or interest would have preferred (March and Olsen 2004). Researchers from this school have found, or posited, that depending on how they are constituted, inclusionary approaches can help foster political legitimacy (Bodansky 1999), public support, and improve policy making (Dryzek 2005; Eckersley 2004; Bernstein 2017; Eckersley 1992). Some strands of this school emphasize how particular engagement might help achieve consensus outcomes (Risse 2000). ***Effectiveness:*** Adherents to the Compromise school assess success as creating some kind of stakeholder inclusionary process that would be required to balance economic, environmental, social and cultural values in ways that produced evaluations of trust, legitimacy and appropriateness of the process (Overdevest and Zeitlin 2016). ***Birds and moles:*** The result was an approach to whack-a-mole effects by striving to balance and compromise across economic, environmental and social goals. However, the school is generally silent on the risks of several external moles. For example, as we will see in Chapter Three, Type 3 stakeholder engagement at domestic and global levels often produce "classic incremental" changes that rarely veer far, and often revert back to, the previous equilibrium. This is often owing to the role of powerful business interests in limiting significant change, while reducing the influence of those whose main objective is to champion environmental values and outcomes (Coglianese 1997). For example, powerful C02 emitting business interests who "…consider climate impacts as harmful but discount them, or consider the costs of climate action as excessive" have been found to be "veto-players" in international climate deliberations (Mitchell and Carpenter 2019, 5). Even when well designed to minimize the role of powerful interests, the compromise school's emphasis on legitimate processes and balance can lead to disastrous consequences for the problem at hand. For example, application of Type 3 multi-stakeholder engagement processes to address overharvesting in the Newfoundland cod fishery a quarter century ago resulted in a compromise decision to allow a harvesting rate higher than what biologists, based on careful attention to the key features of problem at hand, had recommended was required to maintain long-term sustainability (Chase 2003; McKenna 1992). The result was that the Type 1 fisheries economy collapsed, further degrading the Type 4 marine ecology from commercial fishing had already caused (Berrill 1997). To be sure, decisions from this school can produce moles and synergies. For instance, a deliberative approach to a range of interests can produce greater feelings of trust and inclusion that create "peace dividends" that extend far beyond a particular compromise process (Bunse and McAllister 2022). However, this school risks producing, and even supporting, solutions that are inconsistent with the science of what is required to address a particular "on the ground" problem (Cashore et al. 2019b). For instance, Victor (2015), thinking about the agreement rather than its effectiveness, adjudicated the Paris Accord as "transformative" for generating support while simultaneously declaring the 1.5/2°C objectives as "ridiculous. Similarly, the Canadian province of Newfoundland invoked a Type 3 reinforcing compromise dialogue to address biologists' warning that its cod fish were being overharvested. These transparent, trustworthy, and legitimate deliberations led to an agreement on catch levels that were *higher than* what biologists projected would be required to maintain long term sustained yields. The result was the catastrophic collapse of Type 1 g cod fishery, and further degrading those concerned about Type 4 ocean ecosystem degradation (Cashore and Bernstein 2022). Section 19: ##### **iv. Prioritization (Type 4 reinforcing)** ***Problem solving? Yes.*** In contrast, the Prioritization school (Type 4 reinforcing) confronts and interrogates the specific nature of the problem at hand – much the way Ostrom did so for Type 1 problems with her attention to "excludability", this school does the same to understand how, not whether, a particular environmental problem can be solved. For example, my careful attention to this question with Ishani Mukherjee, Lahiru Wijedasa and Altaf Virani, to Peatland, found that a key features was that its key features meant that it was impossible to maintain with irrigation – which would ultimately produce resource collapse (Cashore et al. 2024). Likewise this classification is consistent with those seeking to avoid erosion of hillside streams which the science shows that if policies must conform to the key feature that any number of trees must not be removed (Sabatier and Jenkins-Smith 1993). Hence this school – whether looking at a particular species or the 1.5 degree imperative spends careful time on the nature of the problem, and works inductively, or teleologically, to uncover those key features that would need to be ascertained to effectively manage the problem. ***Utility dominates? No.*** In fact, this school has documented overwhelming evidence corroborating Cashore's Chapter One, that almost all Type 4 problems almost always result from, or are exacerbated by, highly effective Types 3, 2 and 1 policy solutions (Sinden, Kysar, and Driesen 2009; Zeckhauser and Schaefer 1968; Ackerman and Heinzerling 2004). ***Solution orientation:*** confronts "commensurability" biases (Tribe 1972) inherent in the approach of the Commons and Optimization schools to instead, advancing a "sequentialist" approach that ranks "on the ground" problems in order of importance to policy makers and society, rather than turning to cost-benefit or multi-goal policy analysis to make these choices for them (Cashore and Bernstein 2022). Hence, the prioritization school's approach narrows solutions capable of achieving, rather than compromising away, the problem at hand through "fit for purpose" environmental solutions. For example, when it comes to climate they turn to logical causal reasoning by noting that failure to grant the climate and species extinction crises as type 4 challenges risks adopting policy and institutional solutions that that are inconsistent with, or gloss over ecological knowledge: i.e. producing Newfoundlandesque outcomes for the world's most important ecological challenges (IPCC 2018; IPBES 2019). ***Ethical motivations:*** Its moral framework is guided by the belief that some problems are so important that they are incommensurable to others. It draws on rich philosophical and theoretical literatures which reflect on how to distinguish "first order" principles and problem (see, for example, Rawls 1971) from "second order" problems. Tribe (1972, 95) explicitly confronted the "ill fit for purpose" quest of generalizable approaches to policy analysis for first order problems (ibid). The classic example concerns the eradication of slavery in which the very application of economic optimization or compromise approaches as adjudicating frameworks turns analytic attention to assessing, empirically, the pros and cons of owning slaves, which automatically undermines the problem conception and outcome itself: i.e., that no one, for any reason, ought to be allowed to own another human being. This school was drawn on to understand, and inform, the policy, legislative, and agency reforms during the "first wave of environmentalism" in the 1960s and 1970s (Carson 1962; Paehlke 1992), as well the emergence of international attention on the effects of human activity on the natural environment (Willetts 1996; Meadows et al. 1972a). Tribe offered that leading contenders for Type 4 status, included "…vulnerable or ‘fragile'" problems especially "…ecological balance, unspoiled wilderness, species diversity, and the like…[that are]…are intrinsically incommensurable, in at least some of their salient dimensions, with the human satisfactions…" (Tribe 1972, 96). To be sure, Tribe's point was not that all environmental challenges must be conceived of as Type 4 problems, but that policy analysis techniques should not undermine such a possibility: > ... it might well be the case that there is no species whose complete preservation should have lexical priority over all human goods; but it simply does not follow that no minimal level (or perhaps some "overall" level) of species survival or diversity should [not] receive lexical priority over at least some kinds of human benefits ... Tribe offered that it would not “…seem peculiar to insist…that a lower limit be established on especially cruel treatment of animals, whatever the [Type 2] economic gains this cruelty brings to persons in general” (Tribe 1972, 96; parenthesis added). To overcome these commensurability biases, Tribe advocated a “lexical” approach in which the central task of the policy maker is to identify the structural features of the “first order” problem at hand, and then, just Ostrom did for Type 1 commons challenges, inductively identify policies relevant institutional and policy solutions irrespective of “second order” effects (Tribe 1972, 95, 96, 99). Hence, the Prioritization school’s ethical philosophy include the imperative to render explicit hidden conceptions inherent in all policy analyses, and then to consciously, rather than inadvertently, design policies consistent with preferred biases. For these reasons the inherent ethical philosophy behind the Prioritization school is that lower ranked problems—including moles cause by effectively managing higher ranked problems - can only be managed in ways that do not undermine lexical priorities. **Dominant disciplines:** within sustainability studies, the prioritization school has strong roots going back to critiques by biologists, environmental activists, and environmental agencies who collectively posited, and empirically found, that an emphasis on (Type 1 or 2) economic utility tended to be correlated with the degradation of the structure and function of ecosystems and species extinctions (Taylor 1992). It also has strong roots within historically oriented political science, sociology and environmental studies and humanities who, by standing back and viewing trends over decades have been able to view “the forest for the trees” (or perhaps the lack of forests ☹) in ways that more ahistorical leaning disciplines have failed to uncover. **Dominant methodologies:** the prioritization school is most certainly motivated by largely quantitative data, reinforced by extensive field research, that led scientists to alert the world about the ecological crisis at hand (Kütting 2000). It places careful attention on what scientific evidence indicates is required to avoid, or reverse, the effects of humans in general, and commodification of nature in particular, on terrestrial, marine and atmospheric ecological systems. This task requires understanding, and disentangling scientific evidence about the shorter “shelf life” of land-use caused climate change emissions that also threaten biodiversity, from longer term emissions caused by carbon dependent industrialization processes. Members of this school are also keenly aware of the need to avoid conflating data collected by Type 1 commons scholars on “extent of forest cover” or “tree density” from data on the decline of ecosystem structure and function and species extinction risks. However, what is important for this book is that while prioritization scholars are motivated by ameliorating problems that became known through ecological sciences applying largely quantitative methods, they also recognize that, for instrumental reasons, effective policy and institutional solutions require sophisticated training in qualitative methods. This is owing to the recognition Type 4 environmental problems – such as the massive species extinctions and climate crisis – are overwhelmed in political and policy processes that emphasize Types 3, 2 and 1 short-term or material interests (Lockwood et al. 2017; Levin et al. 2012). Hence, the prioritization school must always embrace methods best suited to identifying “critical juncture” policy designs capable of ameliorating, or avoiding, the problem in question (Jordan and Moore 2020). This is because Type 4 environmental problems – such as the massive species extinctions and climate crisis – are overwhelmed in political and policy processes that emphasize Types 3, 2 and 1 short-term or material interests (Lockwood et al. 2017; Levin et al. 2012). An emphasis on critical junctures has led prioritization scholars to draw on path dependency analysis as an innovative way to think about the triggers of transformative change pathways (Peters and Tarpey 2019; Hacker and Pierson 2014). In contrast to the economic optimization school that views path dependency as inefficient or utility undermining (North 1990; David 1985), prioritization scholars embrace path dependency processes to think about how to change policy outcomes, values, and feasibility calculations (Lockwood et al. 2017; Pierson 1993; Hacker 2001). Specific techniques include qualitative “process tracing” and “applied forward reasoning” (Bernstein 2000) with which to project forward multiple unfolding causal steps resulting from a particular critical juncture. Political science research has identified at least two distinct ways in which critical junctures might be triggered: societal and stakeholder conflict¹ on the one hand, and innovative “policy mixes” on the other hand (Howlett 2019c; Peters et al. 2018). The former process cautions political scientists who advocate for stakeholder and intergovernmental processes to be mindful not to inadvertently make decisions that undermines productive societal conflict through the creation of “decoy” deliberative bodies that undermine contestation required for more significant transformations (Dimitrov 2019). The latter turns analytic attention not only to the goals of policy, but the way in which specific calibrations that dictate resources and approaches to compliance, or the specific policy settings that determine behavioural changes, matter not only for identifying micro level “easy to pull”, but “hard to reverse” policy levers capable of producing, over a series of steps, desired results. Given the impossibility of developing generalizable theories about the myriad of possible policy triggers that might be uncovered, this school champions the development of diagnostic design questions with which to guide inductive deliberative exercises for generating policy innovations (Levin et al. 2012; Young 2019). For these reasons prioritization scholars (Hildén, Jordan, and Huitema 2017) caution against the dominance of “experimentalist” policy designs favoured by many self-labelled “data-driven” “empirical social scientists” (Cole 2015) because evidence of transformative effects appears far too late to inform policy design. They are particularly concerned that large-N experimentalist approaches, and learning that results, tend to implicitly bias Types 1, 2 and 3 because empirical evidence for these problems is much more likely to appear within weeks or months following the intervention at hand. It is, arguably for these reasons that climate focused learning experiments tend to focus on human adaptations to, rather than lessons for mitigating, Type 4 environmental changes (e.g. Armitage et al. 2009). For these reasons, the prioritization school requires significant expertise and training in conceptual and reflexive analysis, as well as comparative policy studies, with which to foster inductively generated policy designs (Howlett and Cashore 2009; Howlett 2019b). Owing to concerns of biasing a quest for (Type 2 and 3) universalist theories, quantitative empirical analyses play an important but secondary role in the search for policy designs capable of identifying change levers. It is for these reasons ironic, if not tragic, that GTC and subsequent meta reviews on theories of the policy process championed by Sabatier (Sabatier 1999) and (Ostrom 1999) ignored or dismissed historical institutionalist scholarship precisely because it did not seek generalizable frameworks (Sabatier 1999, 5). ***Theory building project:*** this school turns to two distinct ways in which critical junctures might be triggered: societal and stakeholder conflict¹⁰ on the one hand, and innovative “policy mixes” on the other hand (Howlett 2019b). The latter turns analytic attention not only to the goals of policy, but the way in which specific calibrations that dictate resources and approaches to compliance, or the specific policy settings that determine behavioural changes, can identify “easy to pull” micro level levers capable of producing, over a series of steps, desired results. While these methods are certainly advanced in some strands of policy sciences, they are, as I argue in the introduction to this book, the required analytical capacities to achieve these outcomes are poorly tended to, and with few resources deployed, compared to the enormous set of resources deployed to “double down” and advance Type 3, 2 and 1 reinforcing methods. It is for these reasons that the last half of this book carefully walks us through how to build analytical capacities for integrating the complete range of causal knowledge for advancing Type 4 policy pathways. ***Effectiveness:*** for all these reasons the Common’s school is anchored in achieving desired policy objectives facing a clearly specified, and measurable, “on the ground” challenge whether it is about maintaining the long-term supply of utility enhancing fish for a poverty stricken community, timber management in 1000s of forest dependent communities across India, or long term supplies of clean water facing local urban areas, there is no success, and no sense of accomplishment, if the specified issue at hand becomes degraded or collapsed. This is important because its attention on revisiting theories and prescriptions if they don’t cause the resource good to be maintained (rather than simply managing degradation more slowly) is not only a critically important aspect of this School for Sustainability scholarship policy and governance, its approach to “on the ground” effectiveness in managing the particular Type 1 problem at hand, can, and must, be emulated by Type 4 problems that consumer the interest, and attention, of the Prioritization school. ***Birds and moles:*** the prioritization school recognizes that its sequentialist morality can create “collateral damage” moles. This has led to a critique that ranking environmental problems as highest on the pecking order is “elitist” (Farrell 2020) and even undermines the sustainable development of some of the world’s most poor and vulnerable populations, including indigenous communities and rural livelihoods. However, prioritization school scholars respond it is often possible to grant lexical ordering to the environment that can, indeed, subsequently ameliorate social and cultural challenges (Clapp and Dauvergne 2005), just not in the way envisioned by economic optimization or compromise schools that, in so many cases, result in “lose/lose” undermining outcomes for ecosystems and local cultural traditions (McAfee 1999). While the prioritization school within sustainability studies has thought carefully about how to handle external moles, it has largely failed to explicitly assess the problem of internal moles: i.e., how to conceive of the range of Type 4 problems with which to grant lexical status. Intriguingly, GTC called for such thinking, arguing that while biological and ecological systems were different from those covered in GTC, they, too, could be enhanced by similar inductive attention to the features of the problem at hand species (Ostrom 1990, 25-26). THE "ON-THE-GROUND PROBLEM"** **"On-the-ground problem" refers to substantive, empirically measurable specific policy problems that are: **Specific**: Points to particular events or phenomena, not abstract concepts, or **Teleological**: Analysis begins and ends with solving this specific problem, not validating a universal theory. Examples of TRUE "On-the-Ground Problems": - **Specific environmental crises**: Illegal logging in Amazon forests, overfishing of Atlantic cod, toxic chemical discharge in a specific river - **Measurable targets**: Global temperature rise limited to 1.5°C, reducing traffic accident deaths, preventing extinction of a specific species (e.g., spotted owl) - **Concrete access issues**: Access to K-12 education, clean water supply for urban populations What "On-the-Ground Problem" does NOT mean: **✗ Abstract values/concepts:**- "Environmental conservation" - "Equity" - "Human rights" - "Social welfare maximization"- "Stakeholder consensus"- "Legitimacy" **✗ Policy/governance mechanisms:**- Ecolabel compliance issues- Regulatory enforcement challenges- Top-down policy effectiveness- Information asymmetries - Certification processes- Institutional fragmentation **2. THE TYPE 4 "STRICTNESS" FALLACY** * **The Error:** AI often classifies articles as Type 4 just because they advocate for "strict rules" or "punishment." * **The Rule:** Strict enforcement of a market rule (e.g., timber legality) is often **Type 1** (Commons/Market rules) or **Type 3** (Rule of Law). * **True Type 4:** Type 4 is defined by **Lexical Priority**. It argues that a specific outcome (e.g., ecological integrity) is *incommensurable* and must be achieved *regardless* of economic utility or stakeholder consensus. **3. THE "UTILITY" KEYWORD TRAP** * **The Error:** Classifying as Type 2 just because the word "utility" or "efficiency" appears. * **The Rule:** Determine if the author *adopts* utility maximization as their goal (Type 1/2) or *critiques* it. Do not simply rely on keyword presence. Focus on the *role* of utility in their argument. For example, if they CRITIQUE utility maximization as insufficient, it may indicate Type 3 or 4. ### Article to Analyze Page 1: REGULATION & GOVERNANCE FORUM Orchestrating sustainability: The case of European Union biofuel governance Philip Schleifer London School of Economics and Political Science, Department of International Relations, Grantham Research Institute on Climate Change and the Environment, London, UK Abstract This article provides an empirical analysis of orchestration – that is, the initiation, support, and embrace- ment of private governance arrangements through public regulators – in the ﬁeld of European Union biofuel governance. It examines the emerging sustainability regime and shows that orchestration has been extensively practiced. Regulators in the European Union have used a range of directive and facilitative measures to initiate and support private biofuel certiﬁcation schemes and to incorporate them in their regulatory frameworks. This has given rise to a hybrid regime in which public and private approaches are closely intertwined. Discussing the beneﬁts and complications of engaging with private biofuel sustainability governance, the article’s ﬁndings point to a partial failure of orchestration in this policy area. Keywords: biofuels, European Union, orchestration, private governance, transnational regulation. 1. Introduction Since the Rio Earth Summit in 1992 and the proclamation of Agenda 21, the concept of sustainable development has been at the center of international environmental politics. But making sustainability work under conditions of globalization poses difﬁcult challenges. In the countries of the south, weak administrative capacities are among the main obstacles for achiev- ing sustainability goals. And in the north, where states are strong, regulators remain constrained by national borders and, therefore, ﬁnd it difﬁcult to regulate transnational production net- works.At the same time, states are as reluctant as ever to confer authority to supranational bodies and many intergovernmental forums currently experience stalemates as a result of an increas- ingly heterogeneous international system. In order to overcome these obstacles, it has been argued that public regulators should harness the capacities and competencies of private sustainability governance (PSG) (Abbott 2012). The term PSG refers to the plethora of industry schemes (e.g. Global Sustainable Elec- tricity Partnership), civil society initiatives (e.g. Rainforest Alliance), and multi-stakeholder arrangements (e.g. Forest Stewardship Council) that have emerged in this area in recent years. In a series of articles, Abbott and Snidal (2009, 2010, 2012) argue that public regulators should actively support and embrace these arrangements. They call this “orchestration” and believe that it would strengthen the international regulatory system and help reaching sustainability goals. However, as of yet, this literature remains largely conceptual and normative and there is little real-world evidence about the beneﬁts and potential complications of orchestration. In a ﬁnal section, the beneﬁts and complications of EU biofuel orchestration are discussed. 2. The case for orchestration In a recent article, Abbott and Snidal (2009, p. 511) argue: “states and intergovernmental organizations can and should more actively support and steer [PSG schemes], embracing them as valuable components of the international regulatory system.” They call this “orchestration” and believe that it would strengthen international regulation and at the same time mitigate some of the problems that currently undermine the legitimacy and effectiveness of PSG. The concept of orchestration is deeply rooted in New Public Management theory and the literature on New Domestic Governance. These approaches promote a shift away from central- ized administration toward the use of non-traditional, often private regulatory instruments. Key elements of the new public management agenda are deregulation, outsourcing regulatory func- tions to private actors, the use of market-based mechanisms, and the promotion of competition in the provision of public services (Lane 2000). Thus, calls for orchestration imply a new role for states and IGOs in global governance. In what could be dubbed old global governance, states and international organizations (IOs) were the primary and often only providers of regulation. Rulemaking took place at the national level, through international negotiations and within IOs. Rule implementation and enforcement mostly occurred through the state and its agencies as IOs typically lack direct enforcement powers. In old global governance, public regulators and the subjects of regulation were, thus, engaged in a direct relationship. In contrast, the “key to orchestration is to bring third parties into the governance arrangement” (Abbott et al. 2010, p. 1). The goal is to initiate, support, and coordinate PSG schemes and to integrate them in public regulatory frameworks rather than to govern directly. This is based on the premise that private actors possess resources and have developed competencies and expertise that public regulators can use to better reach their regulatory objectives (see Table 1). However, public and private arrangements often remain poorly aligned. The orchestration literature argues that closing this engagement gap would strengthen the international regulatory system in several ways. One important beneﬁt of orchestration is that governing via intermedi- aries demands fewer public resources. Another advantage is the mobility and reach of PSG. Intergovernmental policymaking processes are often unwieldy and time-consuming and states remain constrained by the rules of the international free trade regime and the transnational nature of production. In this situation, PSG could help to ﬁll in gaps in the international regulatory system and to improve the extraterritorial impact of public regulation (Abbott & Snidal 2009, p. 564). At the same time, states and IOs could use their authority to boost the effectiveness and legitimacy of PSG. One important factor that limits the effectiveness of PSG as a system is its fragmentation and heterogeneity. PSG is the result of the uncoordinated interactions of thou- Orchestrating sustainability P. Schleifer © 2013 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 534 Page 3: sands of groups and organizations from business and civil society. This spontaneous and decen- tralized evolution has produced many gaps and overlaps in terms of issue and industry coverage. As a result, many schemes focus on sectors with highly branded, consumer-facing companies, whereas less visible industries have received considerably less attention. The high concentration of PSG schemes in some sectors is also problematic. The existence of multiple schemes invites forum shopping by companies and increases search and information costs (Abbott 2012, p. 558). PSG’s legitimacy problems are partly a result of the fact that it does not conform to accepted notions of representative democracy. In the transnational realm in which these schemes operate, no clearly deﬁned demos or principal exists with the ability to hold them to account. To generate legitimacy, the concept of deliberative democracy with its focus on participation and transpar- ency has been proposed as an alternative. However, PSG schemes strongly vary in these dimen- sions of institutional design (Abbott 2012, pp. 559–560; Fransen 2012). Abbott and Snidal argue that orchestration can help to mitigate these problems. They propose a number of measures that they subdivide into directive and facilitative orchestration. Directive orchestration relies on the authority of the state. In this regard, states can scale up exemplary initiatives through formally incorporating them in their regulatory frameworks. Furthermore, they can mandate baseline principles, transparency requirements, and other insti- tutional parameters for schemes operating in their jurisdictions. In this way, they can inﬂuence their composition, form, and procedures and, thus, maximize their participatory character and reduce the risk of capture (Abbott & Snidal 2009, pp. 565–573). However, in the transnational realm directive orchestration is often not possible. In particular, IOs often lack direct enforce- ment powers. Through a range of facilitative measures they can still act as orchestrators. Facili- tation is “softer” than directive orchestration and makes use of the full range of governance tools that are characteristic of the New Public Management agenda. For example, as facilitators, orchestrators can provide ﬁnancial support and technical assistance to PSG schemes. They can also endorse initiatives they deem as exemplary and, thus, provide them with legitimacy. In addition to material and ideational support, they can also participate in the formation of new initiatives and, thus, inﬂuence their norms, structures, and procedures (Abbott & Snidal 2009, pp. 573–577).
