However, as of yet, little is known about real-world orchestration, its beneﬁts, and potential complications. In order to address this gap, the remainder of this article describes and discusses the case of EU biofuel governance, a policy ﬁeld in which public regulators have closely engaged with PSG. 3. Biofuel policies and governance in the European Union In the early 2000s, policymakers in the EU started to promote biofuels as a means to reach greenhouse gas (GHG) emission reduction targets, foster energy security, and stimulate devel- opment in rural areas at home and abroad (European Commission 2005). Over the last decade, these policies created a biofuel market with an estimated volume of 14,041 Ktoe or 4.65 percent of total transport fuels (USDA Foreign Agricultural Service 2011, pp. 1–2). With the adoption of Directive 2009/28, also known as the EU Renewable Energy Directive (EU RED), this volume is set to increase to 10 percent by 2020 (European Union 2009).1 However, opinions strongly diverge as to the sustainability of biofuel production. One major issue raised in this debate relates to the carbon intensity of biofuels. The EU promotes biofuels as a means to reduce GHG emissions and, thus, to reach climate change reduction targets. But ofﬁcial ﬁgures often do not consider emissions emanating from direct and indirect land use change. Direct and indirect land use changes are problematic as they can result in an overall negative GHG balance of biofuels. This is the case when, for example, forests, peatlands, or wetlands are converted (Nufﬁeld Council on Bioethics 2011, pp. 32–33). The social impact of biofuels has also become a highly contentious issue. Besides poor labor standards in producer countries and problems with land grabbing, the food crisis of 2007/2008, in particular, sparked much debate about biofuels. As food prices increased sharply during this period, Jean Ziegel, the United Nations Special Rapporteur on the Right to Food, even called them a “crime against humanity” and requested a ﬁve year moratorium on their production (Borger 2008). EU regulators responded to these pressures and criticism by including environmental and, to a lesser extent, social safeguards in their policies. Most signiﬁcant in this regard is the EU RED. The directive contains a sustainability scheme that all biofuels produced or imported to the EU must meet in order to count against the European target. The following subsections provide a detailed description of the emerging public–private biofuel regime. 3.1. European Union level At the EU level, the ﬁrst signiﬁcant step to promote biofuels was Directive 2003/30 on the promotion of the use of biofuels or other renewable fuels for transport (European Union 2003). The directive set an indicative, non-mandatory, blending target of 5.75 percent to be reached by 2010. However, the industry and member states responded only reluctantly to the directive. To further promote the cause of biofuels, the Commission, therefore, formulated the EU Strategy for Biofuels in 2006 and the Renewable Energy Road Map in 2007 (European Commission 2006, 2007). The latter proposed a binding target for the use of biofuels in the transport sector of 10 percent to be reached by all member states by 2020. The European Council endorsed the Commission’s proposal, but demanded that biofuels used for blending need to be produced sustainably (European Council 2007). This and the intensifying controversy surrounding biofuels put pressure on the Commission to amend its proposal. In January 2008, the Commis- sion presented a draft directive in which mandatory sustainability criteria for biofuels were ﬁrst Orchestrating sustainability P. Schleifer © 2013 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 536 Page 5: mentioned (European Commission 2008). Finding the support of the Council and the European Parliament, the proposal was formally adopted as Directive 2009/28 the following year (European Union 2009). Thus, besides establishing a 10 percent target for renewable energy in the transport sector, EU RED also contains a mandatory sustainability scheme. The scheme stipulates that biofuels need to achieve a minimum level of 35 percent savings in GHG emissions when compared to fossil fuels. Another criterion mandates that biofuels cannot be obtained from land that was categorized in January 2008 as having a high biodiversity value. In the same way, biomass obtained from land with high carbon stock cannot be used for biofuel production. It is notice- able, however, that EU RED does not include any social criteria, such as labor and land use rights in its sustainability scheme. Also, food security and indirect land use change are not part of the mandatory criteria. EU RED merely requires the Commission to report to the European Parlia- ment and the Council every two years about the impact of biofuel production on social sustainability and food prices (European Union 2009, pp. 36–38). 3.2. Member state level At the member state level, several governments created national schemes to promote the use of biofuels and to implement the European targets. In the following, the examples of Britain and Germany are brieﬂy discussed.2 In Britain, the government ﬁrst announced its plans to introduce a national blending mandate for biofuels in the transport sector in 2005. Three years later, the Renewable Transport Fuels Obligation (RTFO) came into force. The RTFO established an initial blending target of 2.5 percent for 2008/2009, set to increase to ﬁve percent by 2010/2011. Furthermore, it required fuel suppliers to report on a list of sustainability criteria, very similar to those listed in the EU RED, but also including workers’ rights and land rights (ECOFYS 2007, pp. 6–15). From April 2008, all fuel suppliers in the United Kingdom supplying 450,000 liters or more per annum were obliged to report on the volume of biofuels supplied, as well as their sustainability and carbon intensity. In December 2011, the RTFO was amended to include the mandatory sustainability require- ments set out in EU RED (Department of Transport website). Until 2006, the German government mainly relied on tax exemptions to promote the pro- duction and use of biofuels. It then switched policy instruments and, like Britain, introduced a mandatory quota for biofuels. The Biofuel Quota Act of 2006 mandates an annual 0.25 percent increase in the share of biofuels until a total share of eight percent is reached. Non-compliance is sanctioned through a fee on every liter missing to reach the annual quota (FNR website-a). To implement the European sustainability scheme, the Federal Ministry of Food and Agriculture complemented the Biofuel Quota Act with the Biomass Sustainability Ordinance and the Biofuel Sustainability Ordinance. From 2010 onwards, these policies made the EU RED’s sustainability criteria mandatory for all biofuels produced and used within Germany (FNR website-b, c). 3.3. Private sustainability schemes Alongside the public regulatory framework, about a dozen private biomass certiﬁcation schemes have emerged in the European biofuel industry. Table 2 provides a list of those schemes that have obtained formal EU recognition as qualifying standards (a procedure which will be described in further detail). Initiated by single companies, industry associations, or through multi-stakeholder processes, these schemes have developed principles and criteria for sustainable biofuel production and set up veriﬁcation and certiﬁcation procedures. Essentially, Orchestrating sustainability P. Schleifer © 2013 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 537 Page 6: these systems function as clubs (Potoski & Prakash 2009). Firms striving for membership have to implement the club’s code of conduct in their operations. With the help of professional auditing ﬁrms, club managers then control corporate compliance along the biofuel supply chain. Compliant operators are issued a certiﬁcate that they can use to signal their sustain- ability performance to EU authorities and other relevant external audiences. Among the PSG schemes in the European biofuel industry are industry initiatives like the Biomass Biofuel Sustainability Voluntary Scheme (2BSvs), and multi-stakeholder initiatives like the Roundtable on Sustainable Biofuels (RSB). Some schemes have been explicitly designed to serve as certiﬁers for the European biofuel market, whereas others only later developed standards for biofuel production. Also, there are differences regarding the content of private standards. Whereas all schemes cover the environmental criteria laid out in the EU RED (e.g. a minimum level of GHG emission savings when compared with fossil fuels, a prohibition of biomass obtained from land categorized as having a high biodiversity value or high carbon stock), others include social criteria and, thereby, go beyond EU RED. For example, besides basic labor rights (e.g. right to collective bargaining, prohibition of forced labor), the RSB standard also includes criteria in the areas of land rights, food security, and sustainable livelihoods (German & Schoneveld 2011). 4. Orchestration in EU biofuel governance Based on the preceding distinctions between directive and facilitative measures, this section details the orchestration efforts made by EU regulators. It shows that the Commission, member states, and IOs actively supported, facilitated, and embraced private biomass certiﬁcation schemes. Directive measures In the case of EU biofuel governance, public regulators have taken directive measures at both the EU and the member state levels. To implement the sustainability criteria set out in EU RED, the Commission adopted what Lin (2012) calls a meta-standard approach. Under this approach, economic operators can demonstrate compliance with the EU’s sustainability scheme by becom- ing certiﬁed by a PSG scheme that is recognized as a qualifying standard. A qualifying standard is a scheme that is considered as equivalent to the EU RED’s sustainability criteria. In order to become recognized as a qualifying standard, PSG schemes have to undergo a recognition pro- cedure in which their standards and auditing systems are assessed. Amongst other criteria, a successful application requires independent third party monitoring, a sound documentation system, and evidence of an appropriate skill level of the auditors. Furthermore, it is examined whether a scheme can serve as a source of data on food security and indirect land use change. In July 2011, the Commission concluded the ﬁrst application cycle and formally recognized Biomass Biofuels Sustainability Voluntary Scheme (2BSvs), Bonsucro, Greenergy, International Sustainability and Carbon Certiﬁcation (ISCC), Abengoa RED Bioenergy Sustainability Assur- ance (RBSA), Round Table on Responsible Soy (RTRS), and Roundtable on Sustainable Biomaterials (RSB) as qualifying standards. In 2012, ﬁve further private schemes (Ensus, REDcert, Red Tractor, Roundtable on Sustainable Palm Oil [RSPO], and Scottish Quality Farm Assured Combinable Crops [SQC]) were granted recognition (European Commission website). Economic operators can now use these schemes and their certiﬁcates to demonstrate compliance with EU RED. A second way to demonstrate compliance is through the member states’ national control systems (European Commission 2010, p. 2). PSG schemes have also become an essential part of the regulatory system at the member state level. For example, the RTFO in Britain adopted a meta-standard approach very similar to that of EU RED. In this regard, the Renewable Fuels Agency, which until 2011 was charged with implementing the RTFO, recognized a number of PSG schemes as qualifying standards (Renewable Fuels Agency 2011, p. 39). Similar procedures exist in Germany and the Netherlands. Both countries have now recognized the PSG schemes assessed by the Commission, but have also developed national assessment protocols (Ministry of Economic Affairs 2011). For example, Germany assessed and recognized two PSG schemes (ISCC and REDcert) with a strong national focus a year before the Commission had even concluded its ﬁrst application round. 4.2. Facilitative measures One illustrative example of facilitative orchestration in the biofuel industry is the case of the ISCC. In its founding phase, this scheme was largely funded by the Agency for Renew- able Resources, a special branch of the German Ministry of Food and Agriculture (Federal Ministry of Food and Agriculture 2010). In a similar way, the RTRS has received public grants from the Swiss Secretariat for Economic Affairs (RTRS 2008). IOs have also ﬁnancially sup- ported PSG schemes operating in the biofuel industry. Through its Biodiversity and Agricul- ture Commodities Program, the International Finance Corporation (IFC), for example, provided grants of EUR 214,000 and EUR 71,000 to the RTRS and the RSPO respectively (IFC website). Besides the provision of ﬁnancial support, government agencies and IOs have actively participated in the formation and standard-setting process of some of the schemes. In this regard, the minutes of the RSB Steering Board suggest that the Swiss Federal Ofﬁce of Energy and the United Nations Environment Programme have been active participants of the RSB Orchestrating sustainability P. Schleifer © 2013 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 539 Page 8: process (e.g. RSB 2006). The RSB is not the only voluntary scheme with public participation. The IFC, for example, is a founding member of Bonsucro, and the German Association for Development Cooperation (GIZ) supported the RTRS in its standard-setting activities (GIZ website). In sum, public regulators have used a variety of orchestration measures in the biofuel industry. To implement their sustainability standards, the Commission and several member states adopted a meta-standard approach and formally incorporated PSG schemes in their regulatory frameworks. They also acted as facilitators through participating in some of the schemes and through the provision of ﬁnancial support. 5. Discussion Its supporters argue that orchestration can increase the efﬁciency and reach of regulation and, at the same time, mitigate some of the problems currently undermining the effectiveness and legitimacy of PSG. Using these objectives as a yardstick, this ﬁnal section discusses the beneﬁts and complications of orchestration in the case of European biofuel governance. 5.1. Reducing costs and increasing reach? Probably the primary motivation for EU regulators to engage with PSG was the prospect of saving time and resources. In this regard, a study commissioned by the British Department for Transport discussing the beneﬁts of pursuing a meta-standard approach details: “The fact that proper standards are available and duplication is undesirable thus forms the main reason for choosing to build upon existing standards in a Meta-Standard. In addition to this, it would be impossible to develop a completely new standard within the short time frame available which would still be credible” (ECOFYS 2007, pp. 23–24). At a ﬁrst glance, the path taken appears to have delivered on this objective. After the adoption of EU RED in 2009, it took the Commission only two years to complete the ﬁrst application round for PSG schemes. Almost fully privately ﬁnanced, PSG schemes are now the primary implementation mechanism for EU sustainability regulation in this area. It goes without saying that the adoption of a more traditional command- and-control approach would have required considerably more time and public resources. However, it would be a mistake to assume that private biofuel certiﬁcation does not involve any costs. The development of a standard and certiﬁcation system typically amounts to several million euros. For example, the operational budget of the RTRS for the years 2007–2009 amounted to 1,780,000 EUR, and Bonsucro required some 400,000 EUR to ﬁnance its activities in 2006 (Bonsucro 2006, p. 23; RTRS 2007, p. 7). Given the large number of PSG schemes currently operating in the biofuel industry, their founding and operational costs are likely to be signiﬁcant. Of course, some of these schemes already existed before EU RED came into force. However, many others, for example, 2BSvs, Ensus, Greenergy, ISCC, RBSA, and REDcert, have explicitly been designed to function as certiﬁers for the European biofuel market. This raises questions about the macro-efﬁciency of EU biofuel governance. It appears that the Commis- sion’s decision to recognize any scheme “regardless of whether another recognized scheme already covers the same type of feedstock, area, etc.” has led to the creation of double structures and, as a result, to a highly fragmented system of PSG in this sector (European Commission 2010, p. 2). A closely related aspect concerns the distributional consequences of private biofuel certiﬁ- cation. The meta-standard approach has reduced the costs of regulation for the regulating agencies, but it did not eliminate them. In fact, it seems that most of the operational costs are Orchestrating sustainability P. Schleifer © 2013 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 540 Page 9: borne by ﬁrms located at the upstream end of the supply chain (i.e. producers). Furthermore, the highly fragmented biofuel certiﬁcation market is likely to generate search and information costs for upstream producers. Taken together, these costs could be signiﬁcant and concerns have been raised that they could create barriers of entry for new and small-scale producers in developing countries (Lee et al. 2011; Pacini & Assunção 2011). Of course, similar costs might have arisen under a purely public system. For instance, various national standards would have resulted in high search and information costs for target producers. Making a statement about which system would have fared better is difﬁcult, as this would require counterfactuals. Still, it appears that EU orchestrators could have done a better job by, for example, limiting the number of private standards and, thus, the degree of fragmentation. With regard to the extraterritorial implementation of EU sustainability regulation, relying on PSG schemes has clearly facilitated the extraterritorial impact of EU sustainability regulation. Unconstrained by national borders, private auditing companies are able to monitor compliance in the industry’s transnational production networks. This makes the extraterritorial implemen- tation of EU RED possible, even in countries with low administrative capacities. For example, as of today, the ISCC system has certiﬁed operations in more than 30 countries, among them many developing countries (ISCC website). In contrast, cross-border inspections through the EU or its member states would have been more difﬁcult, if not impossible, to achieve. 5.2. Making private sustainability governance (PSG) more effective and legitimate? The EU’s decision to adopt a meta-standard approach put the Commission in a strong position to shape the emerging system of PSG in the biofuel industry. Its energy section developed an assessment protocol, and in 2010 the recognition procedure was launched. As it was clear from the beginning that only accredited schemes would be able to function as certiﬁers under EU RED, PSG scheme sponsors had a strong incentive to apply. This allowed the Commission to impose a set of baseline requirements. However, it did not set the bar very high. As a result, biomass certiﬁcation schemes continue to differ in signiﬁcant ways. For example, strong differ- ences persist with regard to stakeholder inclusion. Whereas the roundtable initiatives (Bonsucro, RTRS, RSB, and RSPO) involve a broad range of stakeholders in their governance and standard- setting activities, other schemes are signiﬁcantly less inclusive. Above all, this is true for the industry-led initiatives (Ministry of Economic Affairs 2012, p. 24). Strong differences also persist with regard to standards. For instance, German and Schoneveld (2011, pp. 10–14) ﬁnd that – to varying degrees – RSB, Bonsucro, Greenergy, ISCC, and the RTRS include social criteria in their standards, whereas 2BSvs and RBSA contain no such provisions. Furthermore, there is evidence suggesting that PSG schemes continue to differ in their auditing practices. In this regard, the RSB and the RTRS rely primarily on on-site ﬁeld audits, REDcert and ISCC make extensive use of self-declaration,3 and the 2BSvs system even allows for desk audits where a decision about compliance or non-compliance is made on the basis of documentation only. The latter practice is generally considered as problematic, especially in light of evidence sug- gesting that “an audit by 2BSvs can in practice be quite easily fully performed with deskwork with no visit to the ﬁeld for real veriﬁcation – also in high-risk areas” (Ministry of Economic Affairs 2012, p. 28). Differences in stakeholder engagement, standard coverage, and auditing practices are prob- lematic for another reason. By failing to establish a level playing ﬁeld among PSG schemes, EU regulators have created opportunities for forum shopping and, thus, contributed to the onset of adverse competition. In this regard, a comparison of the market uptake of the four multi- feedstock schemes granted recognition in the 2011 application cycle suggests that the more Orchestrating sustainability P. Schleifer © 2013 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 541 Page 10: lenient schemes do considerably better. As of June 2013, 2BSvs had issued 618, REDcert 1224, and ISCC 2801 certiﬁcates (2BSvs website; ISCC website; REDcert website). In contrast, the more comprehensive and inclusive RSB appears to experience problems on the marketplace. Within the same time frame, this scheme only issued seven certiﬁcates (RSB Services website). There are many reasons for slow market uptake, but competition from schemes with weaker standards, laxer veriﬁcation procedures, and lower costs is likely to be a major factor. A closer look at the case of the RSB illustrates a major shortcoming of EU biofuel orches- tration. The RSB is often referred to as the gold standard of biofuel certiﬁcation schemes, praised for its comprehensiveness, inclusiveness, and transparency (Nufﬁeld Council on Bioethics 2011, p. xxvi). In other words, it is the kind of initiative the orchestration literature would want to see scaled up and supported by public regulators. However, in the biofuel case orchestrators have done the opposite. By setting a relatively low baseline for PSG schemes (e.g. making no requirements for stakeholder inclusion and making no references to social stan- dards), they endorsed a model of private governance that beneﬁted initiatives like 2BSvs, ISCC, and REDcert. Legitimized by formal EU recognition, these schemes were able to establish themselves as dominant players in the biofuel certiﬁcation market. This put pressure on the RSB and the scheme is currently considering the introduction of a less stringent entry-level version of its standard. The RSB secretariat justiﬁes this move with:“It is hoped that the 2-tiered approach would avoid the prospect that too many operators decide to get certiﬁed for com- pliance with the EU RED through other certiﬁcation schemes” (RSB 2010, p. 12). This anecdote is indicative of a negative dynamic among PSG schemes in the biofuel industry. It appears that, by setting the bar too low, orchestrators have undercut, rather than promoted, good private governance in this area. 6. Conclusion This article described and discussed the practice of orchestration in the European biofuel industry. Beginning in the early 2000s, EU policymakers introduced blending quotas for biofuels and, in response to concerns about their social and environmental impact, ﬂanked these policies with a set of mandatory sustainability criteria. For implementation, EU regulators primarily rely on private certiﬁcation schemes that they facilitated, supported, and incorporated in their regulatory frameworks. Summing up the above discussion, the case at hand suggests that engaging with PSG schemes can indeed be valuable and allow public regulators to realize some of the beneﬁts discussed in the orchestration literature. In this regard, orchestration provided EU regulators with a cost-efﬁcient implementation mechanism. No resources had to be used to create a public control system and synergies could be realized through relying on existing initiatives. In addition, embracing PSG schemes has facilitated the extraterritorial implementation of EU sustainability regulation. However, the above discussion also revealed that, in other areas, EU orchestration has performed poorly. Most importantly, EU regulators have failed to establish a level playing ﬁeld among biofuel certiﬁcation schemes. This has created opportunities for forum shopping and has trig- gered adverse competition. As a result, orchestration appears to have undercut rather than promoted good private governance in the biofuel industry. One possible explanation for this partial “orchestration failure” is the activities of well-organized economic interest groups. There is evidence suggesting that key producer countries and their industry associations have been very active at the EU level, lobbying the Commission to exclude certain criteria from its mandatory sustainability scheme (CEO 2008).Also, business groups within the EU have proven very capable Orchestrating sustainability P. Schleifer © 2013 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 542 Page 11: of networking and organizing around public biofuel regulation. Learning about the Commis- sion’s plans to rely on PSG in this area, the major players in the European agriculture industry were quick to develop standards and certiﬁcation systems of their own and to lobby the Com- mission for their recognition.4 Of course, this is only a very tentative explanation and more empirical research is needed to investigate the matter further. More generally, the hitherto largely conceptual and normative orchestration literature would beneﬁt from studies with a strong empirical focus. In particular, important insights could be gained from systematically comparing the causes, mechanisms, and outcomes of orchestration across different policy areas, as well as different types of orchestrators (e.g. states and IOs).
